retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: First introduced in 1974 at the University of Glasgow by neurosurgery professors Graham Teasdale and Bryan Jennett, the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) offers an objective method for describing the extent of impaired consciousness in patients with acute medical conditions or trauma. [1] The scale evaluates 3 aspects of responsiveness—eye-opening, verbal, and motor responses. Reporting each component separately allows clinicians to communicate a clear and detailed picture of the patient’s neurologic status. Individual findings from each component may also be aggregated into a total Glasgow Coma Score, offering a concise, though less detailed, summary of overall severity. [2] For example, a score of 10 may be documented as GCS10=E3V4M3, indicating the specific values for each response category.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurologic Exam -- Clinical Significance -- Glasgow Coma Scale
retrieved_docs.content: The Glasgow coma scale (GCS) is a standard to determine the level of awareness in trauma or critically ill patients who present with impaired consciousness. It is a 15-point scale that gives a general idea of the extent of brain injury. [8] The GCS is divided and scored according to eye-opening, verbal response, and motor response.

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The widespread adoption of the GCS began in the 1980s, following the endorsement of its use for trauma patients in the first edition of the Advanced Trauma and Life Support (ATLS) course. In 1988, the World Federation of Neurosurgical Societies (WFNS) incorporated it into its grading scale for subarachnoid hemorrhage. [3] The GCS has since become embedded in numerous clinical guidelines and scoring systems for trauma and critical illness, extending across all age groups, including preverbal children. Required by the NIH Common Data Elements for head injury studies and included in the ICD-11, the GCS is now used in over 75 countries. [4] [5] [6] Since 1974, publications referencing the GCS have increased globally at an average annual rate of 16.7%, accumulating over 37,000 citations. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Clinical Significance -- Glasgow Coma Scale Pupils Score
retrieved_docs.content: The MOST (mortality score for traumatic brain injury) integrates the GCS components, pupillary reactivity, and age. Specifically, MOST assigns weighted values to each component, and based on the sum of the values, a conversion table predicts the mortality risk. The AUC of the model in the United States trauma population was 0.875, outperforming CRASH-Basic (AUC = 0.837) and IMPACT-Core (AUC = 0.821) models. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is a cornerstone tool in the neurological assessment of patients with acute brain injury and impaired consciousness. Since its initial introduction, the GCS has been widely adopted in trauma, critical care, and emergency settings across over 75 countries. This course reviews the fundamental components of the scale—eye, verbal, and motor responses—highlighting scoring methodology, clinical interpretation, and proper documentation practices. Pediatric adaptations, the limitations of the total GCS score, and the use of subscores to detect disorders of consciousness more precisely are also addressed.

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Clinical Significance -- Glasgow Coma Scale Pupils Score
retrieved_docs.content: The Glasgow Coma Scale Pupils Score (GCS-P) was described by Paul Brennan, Gordon Murray, and Graham Teasdale in 2018 as a strategy to combine the 2 key indicators of the severity of traumatic brain injury into a single, simple index. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Clinical Significance -- Classification of Severity of Traumatic Brain Injury
retrieved_docs.content: The relationship between the GCS score and outcome is the basis for the following common classification of acute traumatic brain injury: Severe: GCS 3 to 8 Moderate: GCS 9 to 12 Mild: GCS 13 to 15 The GCS-P score values between 1 and 8 denote a severe injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In both preverbal and verbal pediatric patients, the GCS is an accurate marker for clinically significant traumatic brain injury (ie, injury requiring neurosurgical intervention, intubation for over 24 hours, hospitalization for more than 2 nights, or causing death). [6] The GCS has been incorporated into numerous guidelines and assessment scores, including trauma guidelines (eg, Advanced Trauma Life Support), Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines for severe traumatic brain injury, intensive care scoring systems (eg, APACHE II, SOFA), and Advanced Cardiac Life Support.

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Clinical Significance -- Classification of Severity of Traumatic Brain Injury
retrieved_docs.content: In pediatric head injury, a GCS threshold of ≤5 identifies more accurately the severe injury than the adult cutoff of 8, because pediatric TBI patients with scores of 3 to 5 exhibit markedly higher mortality and morbidity. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Assessment of responsiveness with the GCS is widely used to guide early management of patients with a head injury or other kind of acute brain injury. Decisions in more severely impaired patients include emergent management, eg, securing the airway and triage to determine patient transfer. Decisions in less severely impaired patients include the need for neuroimaging, admission for observation, or discharge. Serial GSC assessments are also crucial in monitoring a patient's clinical course and guiding adjustments in management.

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The reliability of the GCS has been extensively studied. Although its reproducibility has been questioned in a small number of reports, these have proved to be exceptions. Thus, a systematic review of all 53 published reports in 2016 concluded that 85% of the findings in higher-quality studies showed substantial reliability, as judged by the standard criterion of a kappa statistic (k) >0.6. [15] The reproducibility of the total GCS score was also high, with a kappa >0.6 in 77% of observations. An apparent beneficial effect on reliability was observed as a result of education and training. To promote this initiative, a standardized, structured approach to assessment has been established. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Function -- Scoring and Parameters
retrieved_docs.content: The GCS evaluates the following 3 parameters of responsiveness: best eye response (E), best verbal response (V), and best motor response (M). The levels of response in the components of the GSC are scored from 1, for no response, up to normal values of 4 (eye-opening response), 5 (verbal response), and 6 (motor response). The total GCS score thus ranges from 3 to 15, with 3 being the lowest and 15 being the highest.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Neurological Exam -- Technique or Treatment -- Glasgow Coma Scale
retrieved_docs.content: The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is a commonly used system for assessing a patient's level of consciousness. GCS assessment helps determine the severity of traumatic brain injury (TBI) and may be performed by relatively inexperienced care providers in pre- and in-hospital settings. The grading scale, detailed in Table 1, is based on the degree of response in 3 domains: eye opening and verbal and motor function. The latest terminologies are also given in parentheses. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Function -- Disorders of Consciousness
retrieved_docs.content: The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) serves as a tool for classifying disorders of consciousness. A decision tree based on sequential evaluation of GCS subscores enables systematic categorization. This approach designates a recovered posttraumatic confusional state (rPTCS) when the verbal score (V) equals 5 (oriented) and a posttraumatic confusional state (PTCS) when V equals 4 (confused). A minimally conscious state with preserved language (MCS+) is assigned when V equals 3 or motor score (M) equals 6, while a minimally conscious state without preserved language (MCS−) is assigned when M equals 5 and V is less than 3. When none of these criteria apply, the classification assigns a vegetative state/unresponsive wakefulness syndrome if V equals 2 or if eye opening (E) exceeds 1. In the absence of these findings, a coma is designated. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The GCS total score may misclassify the level of consciousness because identical total scores can correspond to different clinical states. A GCS total of 8 can reflect vegetative state, minimally conscious state, or posttraumatic confusional state, and scores of 7 to 11 are associated with 5 distinct disorders of consciousness. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Alternatives to the GCS have been proposed, typically by simplifying its components or incorporating additional features. The Simplified Motor Scale, for example, recognizes only 3 levels of motor response. While this may suffice for binary clinical decisions—such as whether to intubate—in prehospital and emergency settings, it does not outperform the GCS in predicting early mortality. [24] [25] Scales with fewer components inevitably provide less information and cannot match the GCS or GCS-P in distinguishing degrees of early severity, tracking individual patient progress, or correlating with diverse long-term outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), indicating that this form of hemorrhagic brain trauma often co‑occurs with internal injuries and should be evaluated together in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1027
retrieved_docs.content: unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), reinforcing the connection between severe intracranial bleeding and internal traumatic damage. Together, these relationships map a comprehensive hierarchy of general to specific injuries, illustrating how broad injury categories subdivide into distinct anatomical and pathological entities that are relevant for diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2358
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, a corticosteroid formulation, may be employed in the therapeutic management of patients who are in a vegetative state (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة), indicating its potential role in addressing the complex medical needs of individuals with severe impairment of consciousness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2226
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatisch hersenoedeem is a specific subtype of أوديما دماغية, meaning that traumatic brain edema is classified under the broader condition of cerebral edema. As a form of أوديما دماغية, Traumatisch hersenoedeem involves swelling of brain tissue that results from head injury, leading to increased intracranial pressure and potential neurological impairment. Recognizing Traumatisch hersenoedeem as a subset of أوديما دماغية helps clinicians understand its pathophysiology within the context of general cerebral edema, guiding appropriate diagnostic evaluation and targeted therapeutic interventions to reduce brain swelling and protect neurological function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 87
retrieved_docs.content: neurovascular pathophysiology, CSF abnormalities, traumatic head injury, and related cranial nerve disorders, highlighting the importance of accurate classification, targeted procedural planning, and appropriate pharmacologic therapy for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1011
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is classified as an إصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury) and is associated with an إصابة داخلية (internal injury). Both Fracture of anterior fossa and Fracture of middle fossa are identified as types of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, indicating that skull‑base fractures are encompassed within the broader category of intracranial injuries. Additionally, إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is recognized as a subtype of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, further linking closed traumatic brain events to the same injury classification. Together, these relationships illustrate that various traumatic brain conditions—including subarachnoid hemorrhage from an open wound, anterior and middle fossa fractures, and closed head trauma—are grouped under the umbrella of intracranial injury, underscoring their interconnected nature for diagnosis, coding, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Unspecified site injury is a broad injury category that encompasses more specific conditions such as intracranial injury, indicating that injuries without a clearly defined location may include trauma to the brain. This category is primarily derived from the concept of multiple injuries (إصابات متعددة), reflecting that when several injuries occur together or the exact site is not specified, they are often grouped under a general “unspecified site” label. Recognizing that unspecified site injury can represent both intracranial trauma and a collection of multiple injuries helps clinicians adopt a comprehensive assessment approach, ensuring that potential brain involvement is not overlooked and that the overall injury burden is appropriately addressed in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: reinforcing the link between acute vascular events and migraine‑related infarction. Collectively, these concepts demonstrate how neuroanatomical structures, cognitive and emotional functions, imaging modalities, neurosurgical shunt procedures, and a spectrum of vascular, inflammatory, toxic, and traumatic brain disorders interrelate to inform comprehensive diagnosis, risk assessment, and management in neurology, neurosurgery, and neuro‑critical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: reflecting its central role in neurosurgical interventions. Screening for suspected neurological conditions frequently identifies abnormal CSF findings, reinforcing the importance of CSF analysis in diagnosing headache‑related disorders. Head injury (إصابة في الرأس) is associated with negative CSF findings and may lead to olfactory nerve disorder (اضطراب العصب الشمي), illustrating how traumatic brain injury can produce CSF changes and sensory deficits, guiding evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2325
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is identified as a specific manifestation of birth trauma, indicating that brain injuries occurring during the birthing process are encompassed within the broader category of birth trauma. Recognizing this relationship highlights the importance of evaluating neurological complications when assessing and managing birth‑related injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Transitory is described as a temporary medical condition whose clinical course is characterized by غشي, indicating that episodes of drowsiness or altered consciousness are a typical feature of its progression. This relationship underscores that patients experiencing a transitory condition may present with غشي as part of the condition’s natural course.

This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2423
retrieved_docs.content: Paralysis is identified as a member of the SMQ titled “الحالات ذات الصلة بالنَزْف في الجهاز العصبي المركزي والأمراض الوعائية الدماغية” (conditions related to bleeding in the central nervous system and cerebrovascular diseases). This classification indicates that paralysis can be a clinical manifestation of intracranial hemorrhage or other cerebrovascular pathology, highlighting its relevance for diagnosis, coding, and the comprehensive management of patients with neurological deficits associated with central nervous system bleeding.
